items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.q: Why is understanding patronage important for studying historical art?
items.correct: Patrons commissioned art for specific purposes, shaping content and form.
items.misconception: Artists worked freely - patronage was irrelevant to the artwork.
items.why: Students romanticise the lone artist. Most pre-modern art was COMMISSIONED, with patrons specifying subject, materials, even composition. Understanding the commission illuminates choices the artist made.

items.q: Which sculptor is famous for pierced forms?
items.why: It's Barbara Hepworth. “Rodin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Letting material qualities show. “Hiding the medium under paint” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which year did Cornelia Parker make Cold Dark Matter?
items.why: Exact answer: 1991. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
